behaviors.description: Conversation history trimmed to most recent 10 messages when full history exceeds context window.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is a slicing/indexing bug in a single function — taking the last N items from a list. This is entirely self-contained logic; no module handoff is involved. REAL-INFRA: No OS, network, or subprocess. MOCK-HIDE: No LLM or DB dependency needed — pass in a list of 15 message objects and assert the output has 10. LLM-DEP: The trimming algorithm is deterministic. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: escalation would require the bug to only appear when the trimming module hands data to another module; the trimming logic itself can fail without any integration point.
behaviors.test_description: Call the history-trimming function directly with a list of 15 synthetic message objects. Assert the returned list is exactly the last 10 messages in original order. Also test boundary: exactly 10 messages should pass through unchanged, and 9 messages should pass through unchanged.

behaviors.description: The generated response addresses the customer's question rather than producing a generic or off-topic reply.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: Correctness here is entirely about response quality — whether the LLM actually addresses the specific question asked. A stub LLM always returns whatever you hardcode, so a test with a stub will always pass regardless of whether the real model would produce a relevant answer. Running this test 100 times with the real LLM may yield different results. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the LLM hides every possible failure mode for this behavior. MOCK-MISS: No integration-only failure boundary exists here; the bug lives in what the model produces. REAL-INFRA: No OS/subprocess needed beyond the LLM API. MULTI-STEP: Single generation step. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: there is no deterministic in-process check that can substitute for evaluating whether a real LLM output is relevant to the input question.
behaviors.test_description: Send a specific, unambiguous customer question (e.g., 'How do I reset my password?') to the real chatbot pipeline with a real LLM call. Assert the response contains substantive password-reset content and does not consist solely of generic filler phrases (e.g., 'I'm here to help'). Use a small set of representative question types to cover off-topic failure modes.

behaviors.description: The tone re-check correctly identifies when a response is too formal for a casual-tier customer and triggers regeneration.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This behavior spans two modules: (1) the tone classifier that inspects a response and (2) the regeneration trigger that re-prompts the LLM. A unit test on the classifier alone would not catch a wiring bug where detection succeeds but the regeneration is never triggered. REAL-INFRA: No OS/subprocess needed; the LLM for initial generation and regeneration can be stubbed. MOCK-HIDE: The stub LLM can be configured to return a definitively formal response on the first call; the failure being tested is in the detection + trigger routing logic, not in LLM output quality. LLM-DEP: The tone classifier itself is described as a separate flagging step, not an LLM call; a deterministic or rules-based classifier can be tested with a canned formal response. MULTI-STEP: No real LLM steps required. REJECTION-GATE for Agentic: the behavior description does not specify the tone checker uses an LLM — it says 'flagged,' implying a deterministic classifier; the failure mode (misidentification or failed trigger) is in the classifier/routing logic, testable with stub data.
behaviors.test_description: Wire together the tone-checking module and the regeneration-trigger with a stubbed LLM that returns a clearly formal response on the first call. Invoke the pipeline with a casual-tier customer context. Assert (a) the tone checker emits a 'too formal' flag, and (b) the regeneration trigger fires and makes a second LLM call. Also assert the counter-case: a casual response does not trigger regeneration.

behaviors.description: The full exchange (user message, generated response, any regenerations) is persisted to the database with correct timestamps.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure lives at the boundary between the chatbot pipeline and the database layer — a mocked DB would never reveal that timestamps are generated wrong, that regenerated responses are omitted from the record, or that a DB constraint fails. REAL-INFRA: A local in-process test database (e.g., SQLite or in-memory store) is sufficient to reproduce persistence failures; no real network or OS-level IO beyond that is needed. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the DB would always 'pass' the write call regardless of the data shape — field omissions and timestamp bugs would be hidden. LLM-DEP: The LLM can be stubbed; persistence correctness is independent of what the LLM produces. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE for System: a real networked database is not required — an in-process local DB will surface all persistence and schema bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full chatbot pipeline (with a stubbed LLM and a real local/in-memory test DB) for a scenario that includes a tone-triggered regeneration. After completion, query the DB and assert: the record contains the user message, the final accepted response, and the rejected draft; timestamps are non-null, in chronological order, and fall within the test's execution window; no fields are missing or null-coerced.

behaviors.description: When the tone check triggers regeneration, the second response replaces the first — the customer never sees the rejected draft.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the pipeline routing logic that selects which response to surface: after regeneration, does the orchestrator discard the first draft and return the second? This only manifests when the tone-check module, the regeneration module, and the response-delivery module are wired together. REAL-INFRA: No OS/subprocess needed. MOCK-HIDE: The stub LLM can return a formal first response and an acceptable second; the routing bug (e.g., returning the first response object instead of the replacement) is exposed by the integration wiring, not by LLM output quality. LLM-DEP: Replacement logic is deterministic — correctness does not depend on what the LLM says, only on which response object the pipeline selects. MULTI-STEP: No real LLM steps required. REJECTION-GATE for Agentic: the failure mode is a routing/selection bug in the orchestrator, fully reproducible with canned stub responses.
behaviors.test_description: Configure a stub LLM to return a formal response on call #1 and an appropriate response on call #2. Run the full pipeline for a casual-tier customer. Assert the value returned to the caller equals the second (replacement) response, not the first. Assert the first response is not present in any output field visible to the customer. Verify the rejected draft is recorded internally (e.g., in logs or DB) but is not part of the returned payload.
